knowledge_items.title: AI-Extracted Eye-Tracking Features for ADHD Screening: A Tablet-Based Approach
key_findings: AI-extracted eye-tracking features achieve 89% sensitivity and 84% specificity for ADHD screening in children aged 6–14
Tablet-based eye-tracking paradigm successfully distinguishes ADHD from typically developing controls
Saccadic latency and fixation duration are the most discriminative biomarkers
Machine learning classifier outperforms traditional rating scales (AUC = 0.92 vs 0.74)
knowledge_items.methods_detail: Tablet-based eye-tracking with ML classification (random forest + SVM) on 437 children (227 ADHD, 210 controls)

knowledge_items.title: Unraveling ADHD Through Eye Tracking: Pupil Metrics Do Not Outperform Traditional Diagnostic Methods
key_findings: Pupil diameter metrics during cognitive tasks show no significant group differences between ADHD and controls
Eye-tracking based classification accuracy (57% sensitivity) does not exceed that of parent-reported rating scales (61% sensitivity)
Fixation-based metrics show moderate discriminability but high inter-subject variability limits clinical utility
Traditional Conners Rating Scales remain more practical and equally accurate for ADHD screening
knowledge_items.methods_detail: Laboratory-based eye-tracking with pupilometry and fixation analysis, compared against Conners Parent Rating Scales, n=186
limitations: Smaller sample size than comparable studies
Laboratory setting may not reflect real-world screening conditions
Single metric focus without multi-modal integration
